content.text: Why Timelines Matter More Than Targets

Disclosure timelines in vulnerability coordination are not aspirational targets — they are the structural guarantee that creates trust between security researchers and organisations. When a researcher submits a vulnerability report, they are implicitly agreeing to withhold public knowledge of a live risk in exchange for the organisation's commitment to fix it within a predictable window. If the organisation treats the timeline as advisory, researchers have no reason to use the coordinated disclosure channel at all.

Google Project Zero popularised the 90-day standard in 2014. It has since become the de facto expectation in the security research community. Organisations that offer longer timelines are perceived as insufficiently motivated to fix; those that demand shorter timelines are perceived as unrealistic. The 90-day standard works because it is long enough to implement most fixes and short enough to prevent indefinite delay.
The 90-day window starts at receipt of a complete report — one that includes reproduction steps and impact assessment. Incomplete reports that cannot be triaged do not start the clock.
Disclosure Timeline and Remediation SLA Reference

attrs.alt: Disclosure timeline day-by-day decision gates and remediation SLAs by severity

content.text: Critical findings — those involving cross-user data exfiltration, large-scale PII exposure, or exploits that can be automated at scale — require an emergency response, not a sprint cycle. The organisation must:

Implement an interim mitigation within 24 hours. For GenAI applications this typically means disabling the affected feature, restricting the endpoint to an allowlist, or adding an emergency input filter that blocks the known attack pattern.
Assign a dedicated engineer to the permanent fix — not as a sprint item alongside other work.
Page the CISO and DPO within 2 hours of triage confirmation.
Assess whether the vulnerability constitutes a personal data breach under GDPR or equivalent regulation, triggering a 72-hour notification obligation to supervisory authorities.
Notify affected customers before public disclosure where data was accessed.

High-severity findings — system prompt extraction, indirect prompt injection with limited blast radius, insecure tool use affecting single users — are handled as high-priority sprint work. Unlike CRITICAL, they do not require an immediate feature disable, but they do require a dedicated engineer assignment and a fix that lands within the sprint cycle.
The fix must be planned into the next sprint after triage confirmation.
Reporter receives a status update at Day 14 (mid-fix progress) and Day 28 (pre-deployment confirmation).
If the fix slips the 30-day target, the SPOC must notify the researcher immediately with a revised ETA — not wait until they ask.

Medium findings (jailbreaks that bypass safety filters; verbose errors revealing model version) are scheduled patch work: they go into the backlog with a target release date. Low findings (informational, no direct exploitability) are best-effort.

Manual tracking of disclosure timelines across multiple concurrent submissions is error-prone. Automating the SLA tracker against the VDP submission database prevents deadlines from being missed silently.
The SLA tracker should emit alerts at key thresholds so that deadlines are caught in advance rather than missed:
Day -14 (76 days after report): alert if fix is not yet deployed — last window to request an extension or accelerate work.
Day -7 (83 days after report): alert to begin drafting the public advisory and coordinating the disclosure date with the researcher.
Fix SLA + 3 days: alert if fix deadline passed without deployment — escalate to engineering manager and CISO.
Interim mitigation + 24 hours (CRITICAL only): alert if interim mitigation has not been confirmed.

Extensions are appropriate only when a specific, concrete technical reason prevents the fix from being deployed within 90 days — not because the team is busy or the fix is complex in general terms. Acceptable reasons include:
The fix requires a model retrain or fine-tune that has a known minimum turnaround time.
The fix depends on a third-party model provider releasing a new version.
The fix involves an architectural change (e.g. removing a vulnerable agentic capability) that requires customer migration time.
Extensions are not appropriate because the team ran out of sprint capacity, the finding was deprioritised, or the organisation wants more time to assess business impact.

The researcher must consent to the extension — the organisation cannot unilaterally extend. If the researcher declines, the original deadline holds. In practice, researchers who are kept informed and believe the organisation is acting in good faith usually agree to a single reasonable extension.
When Fixes Cannot Be Deployed Before Disclosure
Sometimes the 90-day window expires — with or without an extension — before the fix is deployed. This is not a programme failure if handled correctly. The options, in order of preference:
Deploy an interim mitigation (feature flag, input filter, rate limit) and publish the advisory with the mitigation in place, noting that a complete fix is in progress with an ETA.
Publish the advisory simultaneously with the fix if the fix cannot be deployed before the disclosure date — accelerate the deployment to coincide.
If neither is possible, allow the researcher to publish their write-up and immediately publish the organisation's advisory alongside it, providing user guidance.

What is not acceptable: asking the researcher for repeated extensions while making no visible progress, or claiming the fix is 'in progress' with no specific timeline. Researchers remember these interactions and share them in the security research community.

An unresolved finding disclosed publicly is always more damaging to trust than a resolved finding disclosed alongside a clear advisory. Speed of resolution, not avoidance of disclosure, is the correct optimisation.

The organisation publishes its advisory on the coordinated disclosure date. For web-reachable GenAI applications, the advisory is published to the organisation's security disclosure page (e.g. security.company.com/advisories), a CVE entry if assigned, and notification to any downstream software packages or integrations that embed the affected component.
VDP SLA performance is reported internally on a quarterly basis. The metrics that matter:

Disclosure timelines and remediation SLAs are the binding commitments that give a VDP programme credibility with the security research community. Clear, published SLAs differentiate an organisation that takes vulnerability management seriously from one that treats researcher reports as inconvenient obligations.
The 90-day disclosure standard starts from receipt of a complete, reproducible report — not from when the organisation gets around to triaging it.
Remediation SLAs are severity-tiered: CRITICAL gets 7 days with a mandatory interim mitigation within 24h; HIGH gets 30 days; MEDIUM 60; LOW 90.
Automate SLA tracking with alerts at Day 76 (14 days to deadline), Day 83 (advisory drafting window), and on fix deadline overruns.
Extensions require researcher consent, a concrete technical reason, and a hard cap of 14 additional days — no rolling extensions.
When fixes cannot be deployed before disclosure, publish an interim mitigation advisory simultaneously with the researcher's disclosure rather than delaying.
Track SLA adherence metrics quarterly; an extension rate above 20% or CRITICAL fix adherence below 75% signals a process or resource gap requiring immediate attention.
